data.title: MediWound Ltd: Undervalued High Growth Potential
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am bullish on MediWound Ltd (NASDAQ:MDWD) and rate it a buy. The company is offering investors a rare blend of revenue growth and high potential pipeline. If balance sheet strength is your consideration, this stock fits your criteria as well. The compelling story about this investment opportunity is wrapped up by its undervaluation, with a massive upside potential.

Company Overview

MDWD is a manufacturer and distributor of novel bio-therapeutic solutions for tissue repair and regeneration. Its main focus is enzymatic debridement for burns and chronic wounds. NexoBrid, its flagship product is both FDA and EMA approved as a biologic for eschar removal in both deep partial and full thickness thermal burns. In its pipeline is a promising product, EscharEx, which is in its phase 3 clinical trials.

NexoBrid Commercial Success Driving Solid Revenue Growth

The company’s lead product is demonstrating a notable commercial trajectory. For example, in 2024, NexoBrid generated $20.2 million in sales, up from $18.69 million the previous year. In addition, it is estimated that this year, revenue will hit about $24 million, marking a 19% YoY growth. The main growth driver here is expanding adoption in the US, Japan and EU specifically for pediatric burn care. This follows a publication of phase 3 CIDS data in February 2025, which confirmed NexoBrid’s superiority over other products.

I think this company is set to experience stellar upward momentum in sales because besides its commercial success, its orphan drug status offers market exclusivity, shielding it from generic competition up to 2030.

When I talk about commercial success, I am referring to the 42% growth in hospital orders in Q4 2024. With the current demand exceeding supply capacity, the company is scaling up production with a new GMP-compliant facility. By the end of this year, output is expected to increase sixfold, something I believe will sustain strong revenue growth in the future.

EscharEx, A Promising Product In Phase III

MDWD’s next product is a potential game changer in the chronic wounds market. It is developed for enzymatic debridement of chronic wounds such as the VLUs and diabetic foot ulcers. I have several positives from this product’s milestones. To begin with, it has shown superior efficacy when compared to collagenase in Phase 2 trials. This positions the product as a potential market leader in non-surgical debridement in VLUs.

Further, the value phase 3 was initiated in 2024, an important trial for the product. Results are expected by 2026. I am confident that this product is likely to succeed based on previous data both in efficacy and safety. For instance, 78% of EscharEx patients achieved wound bed preparation, versus 38% of SANTYL, with a 33% wound closure rates compared to SANTYL’s 25%. Given these results, EscharEx has a strong foundation for phase 3 trial success.

If it succeeds, it will address a massive unmet chronic wound market. Take an example of the US alone, where more than 6.5 million patients incur a cost of about $25 billion in treating chronic diseases.

With the rising incidence of diabetes and demand for non-surgical treatment, this product has a high market potential. I find the product’s alignment with the new LCD policy, which requires complement wound debridement before applying tissue substitute, very crucial. This is because it strengthens its market and commercial viability.

In my view, owing to its high prevalence of chronic wounds and the limitation of existing products, this product's approval could drive exponential revenue growth positioning it as a cornerstone for MDWD's long term value proposition.

Strong Balance Sheet

Most investors tend to be bothered by weak balance sheets for growing companies. Interestingly, this company’s balance sheet is very robust. With a total debt of $6.9 million and a cash balance of $43.2 million, its net cash stands at $36.3 million. This not only reflects on its deleveraged status but also its strong liquidity. Notably, its total equity of about $31.2 translates to a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.22 which is an indication of low debt risk.

With technically zero debt(negative net debt), this company’s financial standing is solid. This offers ample runway to fund future developments as well as overcome potential economic headwinds. For example, its net cash is adequate to fund its 2024 R&D expense more than 4x. This implies that the company has approximately a 4-year runway in its R&D expenses. The solid balance sheet reduces the company’s financial risks thus appealing to risk-averse investors.

Market Trends Tailwinds

As the aging population grows, there are rising cases of diabetes and increasing demand for non-surgical treatment options.

These trends have resulted in a rapid growth in the wound care market. It is estimated to continue growing consistently to $28.6 billion in 2028, up from $21.5 billion in 2023.

The company’s product alignment with industry trends such as the shift toward non-surgical interventions and the new LCD policy enhances its competitive market position. As a result, I expect the company to capitalize on these trends for its long term growth path.

Valuation

I think this company’s market cap of about $201 million is low compared to its potential. Given the significant addressable market for its products, I believe that if EscharEx is approved, annual sales would exceed $200 million. This estimate assumes that the company captures about 0.69% of the projected global wound care market size by 2028. Applying a PS of 5x, of course a conservative one given the forward PS of 8.41x, MDWD market cap would be about $1 billion post approval of EscharEx. This is a significant upside potential. Given the promising results in the previous trial stage, I believe it’s very likely this product will be approved.

Q1 2025: Expectations And Areas Of Focus

MediWound is expected to release its Q1 2025 results on May 21. One of the Key metrics to watch is revenue growth. I expect revenue to be in the region of $6 million beating the estimated $5.2 million, driven by its NexoBrid’s accelerating adoption (42% hospital adoption growth in Q4 2024). I am anticipating EPS to be at about -$0.55, a reflection of its ongoing R&D and commercialization efforts.

Alongside financial metrics, investors should pay attention to any updates on its two major products. For the NexoBrid, look for insights about accelerated production expansion, while for EscharEx, look for any updates on phase 3 developments.

Generally, the earnings call is likely to highlight clinical progress, commercialization strategies and financial sustainability. Any positive updates on these key areas could catalyze the stock upwards in the short run and vice versa. As such, I expect some volatility in the coming weeks.

Risks

Although I am optimistic about this stock, several risks warrant consideration:

  • Clinical trial risk: The viability of its product pipeline depends on the outcome of its phase 3 results. Any delays or negative results could impact the stock and delay its financial growth.
  • Regulatory hurdles: Should there be delays in EscharEx FDA and EMA approvals, the company’s growth trajectory would be halted hurting he market sentiments.
  • Economic headwinds: Given the 2.7% economic contraction in the US in Q1 2025 and the rising concerns about healthcare expenditure, MDWD's cash reserve is at risk. Although it mitigates the risk in the short term, a prolonged downturn might affect investor confidence.

Conclusion

I recommend this stock to long term oriented investors targeting a return of about 5x by 2028. This shall be driven by EscharEx’s commercialization and NexoBrid’s expansion. Keep a close eye on phase 3 trial progress for effective risk management.

data.title: Harbour Energy: A Double Digit Dividend Yield From European Natural Gas
data.description:
Introduction

As I have been increasing my position in Harbour Energy (OTCPK:PMOIF) in the past few months, I’m planning to keep close tabs on the company’s performance given the current volatility on the oil and gas markets. I mainly like Harbour’s exposure to European natural gas prices which currently trade at approximately 35 EUR per MWh (which is more than three times higher than the current Henry Hub natural gas price).

Harbour has its primary listing is on the London Stock Exchange where the company is trading with HBR as its ticker symbol. With an average daily volume of in excess of 4 million shares, the London listing for sure is the best listing to trade in the company’s stock. The company’s website contains quite a few ‘download only’ links, but you can find all relevant information I’ll be referring to here.

2025 will be the first full financial year after the large acquisition

While the company doesn’t publish detailed financial results on a six month basis, it does provide quarterly trading updates which allows the market to piece things together. In this article I’ll first discuss the Q1 trading update before moving on to the implications Although those are not representative of the new, larger Harbour Energy, it does provide a few useful elements.

In the first quarter of the current financial year, Harbour Energy produced approximately 500,000 barrels of oil-equivalent per day with approximately 60% of the oil-equivalent output consisting of natural gas. 40% of the output was classified as liquids while another 40% was natural gas sold at the [high] European prices with the remaining 20% of the production mix consisting of non-European natural gas. The country with the largest contribution to the production mix is now Norway, which represents in excess of a third of the oil-equivalent production.

The unit costs continued to decrease thanks to the strong operational performance and the low-cost (and gassy) nature of the assets that were owned by Wintershall DEA which was acquired by Harbour. The production cost decreased from $18/boe to just $13/boe, resulting in healthy margins.

It’s perhaps also worth emphasizing the company is still actively managing its hedge book and for 2025, it has hedged about 18 million barrels of oil at $75/barrel while the natural gas hedges were priced at $12.5/mscf. This year’s hedges cover approximately 40% of the anticipated natural gas and liquids production.

And even for next year, the hedges look fine with decent oil hedges at US$73/barrel although the natural gas price will drop to $10.5/mscf on a hedged basis but it goes without saying that the higher market prices will compensate for the lower hedging price, and Harbour Energy remains exposed to the strong European natural gas market.

The strong performance in the first quarter also allowed the company to tighten a few elements of its full-year guidance. The average production rate will now come in at 455,000-475,000 boe/day (the lower end of the guidance was increased by about 5,000 boe/day) while the total capex was reduced as well to $2.4-2.5B (coming from $2.4-2.6B as shown below). The total capex will trend down in the next few years as from 2026 on the average annual capex will decrease to $1.7-2B per year, and this step down could boost the net free cash flow by 30-40% per year.

The lower oil and natural gas prices had a negative impact on the anticipated free cash flow result, and the company now expects to generate $900M in net free cash flow, based on the midpoint of the production guidance and the capex guidance. This is based on an average Brent oil price of just $68/barrel and considering Brent was $75 in Q1, this basically implies a Q2-4 2025 Brent oil price of around $65/barrel. That’s also much higher than the company’s internal threshold to commit to the construction of a project. The company targets IRR’s above 20% while the breakeven point for new project has to be achieved at an oil price below $40/barrel and a natural gas price below $5/mscf.

And as the image above shows, Harbour Energy will still have to pay billions in taxes. The total tax bill is now estimated at in excess of three times the net free cash flow and it is no secret Harbour Energy is still actively looking to exit its UK operations as the tax structure is just very prohibitive.

Most of the larger investments are indeed occurring outside of the UK. The company has taken a final investment decision on the 15% owned Southern Energy LNG project in Argentina where its local natural gas will be liquefied. Additional efforts to increase production in Mexico and Indonesia are underway.

Meanwhile, Harbour Energy remains protective of its balance sheet. The company has an investment grade rating from both Moody’s and Fitch as the net debt decreased to $4.2B. It should be possible to reach a net debt position of $4B by the end of this year (the net free cash flow in the next few quarters will be relatively low due to the timing of investments and tax payments. With an EBITDAX (EBITDA before exploration) of $4B in 2024 (and this will increase sharply this year considering the acquisition contributed just a few months to the FY 2024 EBITDAX result), the debt level appears more than acceptable.

Takeaways

I have been increasing my long position in Harbour Energy in the past few weeks and months as it is one of the easier ways to gain exposure to the strong European natural gas prices. The dividend policy is quite generous: at the current share price the implied dividend yield is 11% (using the company’s policy to pay out $455M per year in dividends) at a payout ratio of approximately 50%.

I hope the company can exit its UK operations and redeploy capital in areas and regions where the fiscal regime isn’t as prohibitive.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: Inference Is Hitting An Inflection Point
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig.

Investment Thesis

Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) shares are up almost 5% since I last wrote on the company in February despite an overall sliding stock market. However, shares are down nearly 4.8% year-to-date, and they’re down more than 23.14% in the last year. While price action has gone in the wrong direction overall over the last 12 months, I remain bullish on AMD, and I still think shares have strong upside potential on the back of inference demand starting to shine. I think the market has started to notice this since February. Given the company’s improving fundamentals, I view this year's long pullback as a buying opportunity. Recent trends since earnings have only solidified what, I think, is the company’s strong foundation.

I see AI inference emerging as a major growth driver. I believe that hyperscalers will spend an estimated $371 billion on data-center compute in 2025 (up 44% year-over-year) and by 2032, nearly half of AI spend will shift to inference. In essence, AI inference spending is a growing market that AMD is uniquely positioned to take advantage of.

While their growth is slower than the triple digit growth the market saw from their competitor Nvidia (NVDA) last year, I believe AMD has some of the best data center momentum of any tech company out there. In Q1, AMD’s data center revenue surged 57% YoY to $3.7 billion. This growth was driven by CPU share gains and “strong growth of AMD Instinct GPUs” for AI workloads.

With their frankly unique ecosystem of leading CPUs, GPUs and adaptive chips, I think AMD could more than double its AI data-center revenue in the coming years. Wall Street forecasts ~20% annual revenue growth through 2026 (to about $37.4 billion) and overall ~60% EPS growth in 2026 reflecting this optimism. I actually think these assumptions are conservative.

The era of inference AI computing is here. With this, I remain a strong buy on shares of AMD.

Why I’m Doing Follow-Up Coverage

While earnings were last week, I want to point out that this is not just a Q1 recap. Although AMD did report what, I thought, were strong Q1 figures the week ago Tuesday, this article goes beyond rehashing quarter-end figures. I want to focus on how Q1 confirmed what, I think, is an AI inflection. I think the devil is in the details here.

After AMD posted a slight data-center miss in Q4 2024, the company delivered $7.44 billion in revenue (+36% YoY) and $0.96 EPS in Q1, beating estimates and prompting management to highlight “very strong adoption” of new AI products. Wall Street has been worried that Nvidia is doing too well and leaving little space for AMD in the AI GPU market. I believe this cannot be further from the truth.

I think the ramp of Instinct MI325X shipments and commentary on double-digit AI revenue growth are clear indicators that inference demand is hitting a turning point. I believe AMD is the clear leader in this section of the market. Shares deserve a higher forward P/E multiple. This is why I am doing follow-up coverage.

Inference At An Inflection Point

One of the things that excites me most about AMD is that inference demand appears to be surging. I think we are reaching a real turning point.

During a week ago Tuesday’s earnings call, AMD management noted that the company experienced “significant double-digit” AI revenue growth as MI325X GPUs ramped across 35+ cloud platforms.

Turning to our data center AI business, revenue increased by a significant double-digit percentage year over year, as MI325X shipments ramp to support new enterprise and cloud deployments. More than 35 MI300-series platforms are in production from all the leading service providers, supporting the expanding number of Instinct GPU deployments with cloud, enterprise, and AI customers.

Recent breakthrough models like OpenAI’s “o3” and even DeepSeek’s (DEEPSEEK) R1 require far more compute per query, shifting spend from training to inference. I believe it’s clear that AMD is capturing far more of demand than they did during the pre-training part of the AI cycle.

I think AMD’s MI300-series is a huge player right now. MI300X’s 192GB HBM advantage (compared to Nvidia’s 80GB) enables single-GPU inference of 66B-parameter LLMs and attracts Meta and Microsoft for large-scale deployments.

Microsoft and Meta are not new customers. What matters is that inference compute is growing fast enough where they should grab wallet share at these key hyperscalers.

So I think overall what we're seeing is with these new reasoning models, the inferencing is more important. And there is also a move to more distributed inferencing. So, I think that plays into our strengths. I think, we have demonstrated that with MI300 that we are an excellent inference solution. And that holds true for 355 and 350 series as well.

In my opinion, AMD’s shift to bi-weekly ROCm releases, day-zero support for LLaMA 4, GEMMA 3, and R1, and over 2 million Hugging Face models now running on AMD hardware means their ecosystem is spreading exponentially.

Really, this is the inflection point for inference.

I think AMD’s memory and interconnect advantages with their complete CPU to GPU ecosystem position the company in a great position. I think this call confirmed what the market is already showing: inference has reached its inflection point.

Adding to this, we’re starting to see key AI startups like TensorWave power their strong growth primarily on AMD architecture. TensorWave this week announced a $100 million Series A financing round.

The GPUs they use? 8,000 of AMD’s MI325X series.

I think this funding round shows how a key part of the future of inference will be done on AMD GPU’s.

Valuation

AMD has a very strong growth outlook. Consensus estimates see FY2025 revenue of $31.62 billion (+22.4%) and FY2026 revenue of $37.29 billion (+17.94%), with EPS rising from $4.01 to $5.95. In my opinion, I actually think these figures are conservative if inference continues to accelerate.

Right now, AMD shares sit around $117 per share (even despite the impressive buyback program announced this week). I believe there is room for multiple expansion across the board with most valuation metrics. Looking at AMD’s forward PEG ratio, their current forward valuation comes in at 1.10, which is almost 36% below the sector median of 1.72. This is also 13.79% below the company’s five-year average of 1.28. Seeking Alpha Quant gives AMD a grade of an A- on this metric.

On this same front, the company has a forward non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio of 28.02. Although this is actually currently higher than the sector median of 22.33, this is still a 31.32% difference to the company’s five-year average of 40.81. AMD earns a grade of a C on this metric.

AMD’s Q1 gross margin hit 54% (+140 bps YoY) in Q1. I really think the GPU maker is starting to fire on all cylinders. The market should reward them for this.

With this, I believe that shares should trade at the sector median forward PEG ratio. If we saw this happen, this would imply 56.36% upside in shares.

Risks

While I've been bullish on AMD, no company comes without at least a few risks. One of my bigger concerns is that the company has to execute near flawlessly on their software/ecosystem execution. AMD has to continue to narrow the CUDA gap because while CUDA is less valuable in an inference environment (vs. a pre-training cluster), any lag coming from their ROCm driver quality or their framework support will likely mean slow adoption despite what, I think, are clear hardware advantages for inference and TCO (total cost of ownership). AMD has a clear path to show they are the cheaper option in the inference space. But in an industry that is still flooded with funding, cost is not everything.

The other big risk I am concerned about are potential export controls on their GPUs to China. According to Reuters, U.S. curbs forced a $1.5 billion revenue hit in 2025. I think if the trade war continues to wreak havoc between two of the world’s biggest economies, we're going to see AMDs top-line growth come in slower than expected.

The silver lining here is that the Trump administration is looking to go the other way. Just in the last three days, the administration has explored rewriting the Biden era chip restrictions. AMD stock has rallied on this. I think these overhauls destroy one of the biggest bear theses.

Bottom Line

While this research is not just about Q1, if we learned anything from the quarter, it’s that AI inference is finally at a pivotal moment for AMD. Not only is it pivotal, it's here now.

If AMD can dominate in this sub AI GPU field, I believe they will be incredibly successful against competitors like Nvidia.

With this, I think AMD has an attractive risk/reward ratio. Less than one year ago, shares were worth over $180. Since then, they’ve dipped all the way down to $115/share range. I continue to believe AMD offers AI upside at an extreme discount (compared to peers), and their continued execution (led by CEO Lisa Su) should drive a forward PEG multiple expansion as the inference story unfolds. I think it would be a huge-missed opportunity to miss out on a company with strong high AI upside like AMD at such a large discount. Really, I think the last few weeks have pushed the market to think differently about AMD. Shareholders should be rewarded.

With this, I am sticking with my strong buy stance from February.

data.title: Meta Platforms And The Five Growth Opportunities
data.description:
In my last article I argued – similar to my previous article – that Meta Platform’s (NASDAQ:META) stock price was a little too high and I rated the stock once again as a “Hold”. And since my last article, the stock declined 15% and from its previous all-time high the stock declined 34.2% in the first few months of 2025. In the conclusion of my last article I wrote:

I still see Meta as a great business, and it certainly has the potential to grow in the double digits over a long time. But trading for a valuation multiple of 30 is making the stock no bargain, which means I will hold on to my Meta shares but won’t increase my position right now.

One can argue that it is already too late to buy Meta Platforms. In the last 10 trading days, Meta Platforms already increased from about $480 in mid-April to almost $600 again. Nevertheless, let’s look at the company and stock once again and determine if the stock might be a “Buy” again or if investors should still hold on to the stock or take the opportunity and try to get out.

Quarterly Results

As often in my articles, I start by looking at the last quarterly results. On April 30, 2025, Meta Platforms reported first quarter results and it did beat expectations for revenue as well as earnings per share. Earnings per share were $1.21 higher than analysts expected, and revenue was $950 million higher.

When looking at the year-over-year comparison, revenue increased from $36,445 million in Q1/24 to $42,314 million in Q1/25 – resulting in 16.1% year-over-year growth for the top line. Income from operations increased 27.0% year-over-year from $13,818 million in the same quarter last year to $17,555 million in this quarter. And finally, diluted earnings per share increased from $4.71 in Q1/24 to $6.43 in Q1/25 – resulting in 36.5% YoY bottom line growth.

While Meta Platforms reported great results on its income statement, free cash flow declined 17.5% year-over-year from $12,531 million in the same quarter last year to $10,334 million this quarter.

When looking at the different segments, the picture is still similar to previous quarters. The biggest part of revenue is still stemming from the Family of Apps and especially advertising is driving revenue growth. Family of Apps increased its revenue by 16.3% year-over-year to $41,902 million and advertising revenue increased 16.2% year-over-year to $41,392 million. “Other” generated a revenue of $510 million in Q1/25 growing by 34.2% year-over-year. This segment is mostly the WhatsApp Business and also Meta Verified subscriptions.

The second major business segment is Reality Labs, which continues to be a disappointment. In Q1/25, the segment generated $412 million in revenue and compared to $440 million in the same quarter last year this is a decline of 6.4% year-over-year. The segment once again reported an operating loss of $4,210 million (compared to an operating loss of $3,846 million in the same quarter last year).

Aside from the segment results, we can also look at some additional metrics for Meta Platforms. The number of daily active people increased from 3.24 billion in Q1/24 to 3.43 billion in Q1/25 – resulting in a 5.9% year-over-year increase, which is still a solid user growth.

Meta Platforms also increased the average price per ad by 10% year-over-year and the number of ad impressions also increased by 5% year-over-year. While the Asia-Pacific region increased the average price per ad only by 3%, US & Canada increased the average price per ad by 14% and the “Rest of World” increased even 17%.

Growth

One of the most important questions for Meta Platforms is whether the company will be able to continue growing at a high pace. As we will see later, Meta Platforms must continue growing in the double-digits for the current stock price to be justified and during the last earnings call, management – especially Mark Zuckerberg – outlined several growth strategies.

One growth opportunity Mark Zuckerberg was talking about in more detail is more engaging experiences. And one opportunity that is not monetized so far is Threads, which has been performing quite well. The app has now 350 million monthly active users and, according to Zuckerberg, it is on track to become the next major social app in Meta’s portfolio. And although management does not expect Threads to be a meaningful driver of overall impressions or revenue growth, the company started to introduce ads on Threads and opened it up to all eligible advertisers to reach people in over 30 different markets (including the United States).

But not only has the time spent on Threads increased 35% in the last six months, in the case of Instagram it increased 6%, and in the case of Facebook, it increased 7%. And Mark Zuckerberg underlined how AI will help the company:

In addition to better recommendations for existing content types, AI is also enabling the creation of better content as well. Some of this will be helping people produce better content to share themselves. Some of this will be AI generating content directly for people that is personalized for them. Some of this will be in existing formats like photos and videos, and some of it will be increasingly interactive.

Another growth opportunity is Meta AI. Across the different apps, there are now almost a billion monthly active users for Meta AI. Aside from including Meta AI into the different applications and platforms (with WhatsApp seeing the strongest user interaction with Meta AI), Meta Platforms also released the first Meta AI standalone app, which is personalized and has a social feed included. Currently, Zuckerberg is not planning on monetizing Meta AI, but is first trying to deepen user engagement:

Earlier this year, we began testing the ability for Meta AI to better personalize its responses by remembering certain details from people's prior queries and considering what that person engages with on our apps.

We are already seeing this lead to deeper engagement with people we've rolled it out to, and it is now built into Meta AI across Facebook, Instagram, Messenger, and our new standalone Meta AI app in the U.S. and Canada. We're also continuing to focus on helping people connect over content.“

A third growth opportunity are AI devices and Zuckerberg is seeing glasses as the ideal form factor for both AI and the Metaverse because:

They enable you to let an AI see what you see, hear what you hear, and talk to you throughout the day, and they let you blend the physical and digital worlds together with holograms.

Right now, more than a billion people worldwide wear glasses already and Zuckerberg is expecting that these glasses will become AI glasses over the next 5-10 years. Apparently, Ray-Ban Meta AI glasses sales are growing at a high pace and tripled in sales. But once again, it remains to be seen if sales will really take off and become a mass market. Additionally, competitors will come into the market if the strategy is successful. But after Meta manages to establish Threads (after the company failed several times before to introduce new apps), maybe Meta Platforms will also be successful and make hardware sales a huge part of its business in the years to come.

A fourth growth opportunity is business messaging, which I have mentioned already in several previous articles. And when looking at “Other” revenue – which is mostly business messaging – we see solid growth rates. Right now, it is only responsible for 1.2% of total revenue and won’t therefore be a major driver of overall growth. However, Zuckerberg sees growth potential in the years to come:

In countries like Thailand and Vietnam, where there is a low cost of labor, we see many businesses conduct commerce through our messaging apps. There's actually so much business through messaging that those countries are both in our Top 10 or 11 by revenue, even though they're ranked in the 30s in global GDP. This phenomenon hasn't yet spread to developed countries, because the cost of labor is too high, to make this a profitable model before AI, but AI should solve this.

The fifth growth opportunity Zuckerberg mentioned during the earnings call is improved advertising. But as Meta Platforms is constantly trying to improve advertising, I won't go deeper here.

And analysts are also quite optimistic for the years to come and expect earnings per share to grow with a CAGR of 12.2% between fiscal 2024 to fiscal 2030. Since 2022, analysts are constantly raising targets for earnings per share and only in the last few months analysts were getting a little more pessimistic again.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

Finally, we are looking at the valuation of the stock and we start once again by looking at the price-earnings ratio and the price-free-cash-flow ratio. At the time of writing, the stock is trading for 25 times earnings, and this is slightly below the 5-year average of 25.23. Meta Platforms is also trading for 31.8 times free cash flow, which is above the 5-year average of 27.56.

Looking at these valuation multiples, we can conclude that Meta Platforms is probably fairly valued and trading at reasonable valuation multiples, which are in line with past numbers. And when looking at the Magnificent 7, only Alphabet is trading for a lower valuation multiple (27.37 times free cash flow at the time of writing). Of course, we can’t just compare these 7 companies and assume that these seven companies should all trade for similar valuation multiples. Nevertheless, it is indicative that Meta Platforms, which is certainly growing at a high pace, is not among the most expensive stocks.

Aside from looking at simple valuation multiples, we can also use a discount cash flow calculation to determine an intrinsic value for the stock. As always, we are calculating with a 10% discount rate as this is the annual return on investment we would like to achieve at a minimum. Additionally, we are calculating with 2,590 million in diluted shares outstanding. As basis for our calculation, we can use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, which was $52.31 billion.

In order to be fairly valued, Meta Platforms must grow its free cash flow between 13% and 14% annually for the next ten years followed by 4% growth until perpetuity. Let’s look at these assumptions in more detail.

First, the free cash flow of the last four quarters seems like a reasonable basis we can use in our calculation. In the last four quarters, 30.7% of revenue ended up as free cash flow and this is in line with the long-term average of 31.6%.

But I think Meta Platforms is a high-quality business with a wide economic moat around its business and it seems like a business that will be able to grow with a much higher pace for a long time (several decades). And therefore I would calculate with 6% growth till perpetuity.

However, I don’t know if 14% growth for the next ten years is reasonable and maybe we should be a little more cautious. We calculate with 12% growth for the next ten years, which are still very optimistic assumptions and are in line with analysts’ assumptions for the years to come. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $771.61, and the stock might still be a good buy at this point.

However, we did ignore one important aspect so far – the potential risk of a recession.

Recession Risk

During the last earnings call, the word “recession” was not mentioned and according to a sentiment analysis of the earnings call transcript, management is rather optimistic and confident about long-term growth. And of course, we should always think in probabilities and a recession in the coming quarters is only one of the possible scenarios.

Nevertheless, it is a scenario we should prepare for as the probability (or: risk) is quite high – not only due to the tariffs and turmoil it generated. On the surface it might seem like tariffs are not such a big issue anymore, but we should not forget that on the one hand, high tariffs are only suspended while we still have tariffs of at least 10% on most goods. On the other hand, the economy (and financial markets) are built on trust and an administration being completely erratic might not be helpful in the long run.

Meta Platforms can be seen either as a technology company or a communication services company. And especially technology stocks were hit hard in most previous recessions (in part due to the high valuation multiples these stocks are usually trading for). And on the one hand we can argue that user engagement might even improve during a recession and the number of monthly or daily active users will improve as well as the time spent on Meta’s platforms. When more people are unemployed, they usually have more time to fill and one way is to spend more time in front of the TV or screen (this can be shown by increased gaming time during COVID-19 or the time watching TV increasing during the Great Recession). Of course, the screentime – especially among young adults – is already very high and constantly increased during the last few years raising the question if it will increase much more during a recession.

However, Meta Platforms is not just dependent on people using the platform and seeing potential ads. The company is also dependent on companies spending money on ads (as this is the way Meta is generating most of its revenue – see section above). And in case of a recession many companies must cut marketing budgets (although this might not always be the smartest move and certainly not the best long-term strategy).

Another problem we as investors are faced with is that we don’t know how Meta Platforms has usually performed during recessions, as we don’t have much useful data. The company has seen two recessions so far, but in both cases the numbers are not really useful. During the Great Financial Crisis, the company was a very young startup, and we have limited data and extremely high growth rates (which is typical in such early phases of a business).

The second recession was the COVID-19 recession in 2020, and this was rather an untypical recession, and we should be careful to draw conclusions from the data we have. Meta Platforms was one of the companies profiting from the stay-at-home orders as people were spending much more time online.

Conclusion

In my opinion, Meta Platforms remains a “Hold”. On the one hand, the business has long-term growth potential and with its wide economic moat around the business I still see it as one of the best business models out there. Nevertheless, we also must see the stock fairly valued at best and the stock price is probably not reflecting the risk of a recession and maybe lower growth in the next few years.

Although this is not really helpful now, buying Meta Platforms when it dropped below $500 a few weeks ago might have been an acceptable investment decision. But with the stock trading for $660 again, I don’t know if now is the time to purchase. While I don’t want to buy any shares of Meta, I will certainly hold on to my position as I think the company is a great long-term “Buy”.

Russell 2000 Growth Index measures the performance of those Russell 2000 companies with higher price-to-book ratios and higher forecasted growth values.

Small Cap Composite - 1Q25 Performance & Attribution

The Conestoga Small Cap Composite (MUTF:CCASX) declined -11.35% net-of-fees in the first quarter of 2025, modestly underperforming the Rusell 2000 Growth Index decline of -11.12%. While Conestoga's other high-quality growth strategies were able to outperform their benchmarks in the first quarter's sell-off, the Small Cap Growth portfolio had several unique holdings which experienced company-specific issues and generated weaker relative returns. These stocks generally impacted our relative performance in the first half of the quarter as the market rose and peaked in mid-February. As the market declined over the second half of the quarter, we were pleased to see that the client portfolios did outperform, as we would expect. However, the second half performance recovery was not quite enough to close the gap versus the benchmark.

Traditional attribution analysis reveals that sector allocation effects were modestly negative over the quarter, while stock selection effects were slightly negative. From a sector allocation perspective, an underweight to Consumer Discretionary and an overweight to Utilities added to relative returns. An overweight to Technology and an underweight to Financials, Health Care and Consumer Staples detracted from relative returns.

The Industrials sector proved the most challenging from a stock selection perspective and was home to a few of the unique Small Cap Growth holdings that caused underperformance relative to our other strategies. AAON, Inc. (AAON), a manufacturer of specialty heating, ventilation, and air-conditioning (HVAC systems), saw its stock decline as the industry adjusted to new refrigerant standards and the company absorbs additional expenses of expansion plans which delayed deliveries. Transcat, Inc. (TRNS), which provides calibration services to labs, reported weaker-than-expected revenue and earnings growth. Not unique to Small Cap Growth, but also among the detractors in Industrials, was Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD). We attribute the weakness in ROAD to profit-taking after a very strong 2023 and 2024, as well as a short report released in late January.

Health Care was another source of weaker stock selection effects. Azenta, Inc. (AZTA) saw its stock decline as concerns about the future funding from the National Institute of Health (NIH) and China exposure impacted many bioprocessing companies. Neogen Corp. (NEOG), which provides food and animal safety products, continued its recent declines as it works through the integration of the acquired food safety business from 3M Co. (MMM).

Stock selection was strongest in the Technology and Basic Materials sectors. Within Technology, payroll and human capital management company Paycor HCM, Inc. (PYCR) rose over 20% at the beginning of the year with the announcement that the company would be acquired by Paychex, Inc. (PAYX). In the Basic Materials sector, long-time holding RBC Bearings, Inc. (RBC) reported better-than-expected quarterly revenues and earnings for its specialty components business.

Small Cap Composite - 1Q25 Top 5 Leaders

ESCO Technologies, Inc. (ESE)

ESE is a diversified industrial that provides diagnostic equipment to utility companies, filtration/flow devices to the aerospace/defense industry and develops testing equipment for the electronics/telecommunication segments. ESE reported strong Q4 results with a nice recovery in its Test segment with orders in the quarter up a 43% year-over-year. Also, the recently announced acquisition of SM&P, which expands ESE's defense business, has been very well received by the market.

Paycor HCM, Inc. (PYCR)

PYCR shares outperformed after Paychex announced its all-cash acquisition offer at $22.50 per share, representing a 22% premium to the prior day's close. Before the announcement, the company delivered Q2 results with core recurring revenue growth of 17% year-over-year and modest gross margin expansion. PYCR's AI chatbot continued gaining traction since its October launch. The broker channel remained a key differentiator, contributing 25% of new business. With Apax Partners (a 53% shareholder) supporting the transaction, the deal is expected to close in April 2025.

Merit Medical Systems, Inc. (MMSI)

MMSI delivered strong quarterly results, exceeding expectations for both sales and earnings growth. The company's vertically integrated business model continued to provide manufacturing stability and competitive advantage in both cardiovascular and endoscopy segments. Its OEM segment continued to resonate with customers' persistent supply chain challenges in the industry. Gross and operating margins showed consistent improvement, positioning MMSI well to achieve its longterm targets. We believe recent product launches along with strategic acquisitions enhance MMSI's position in higher-growth therapeutic markets.

Casella Waste Systems, Inc. (CWST)

CWST is one of the largest solid waste services companies in the Northeast, uniquely positioned with excess landfill capacity in a capacity constrained region. CWST, along with most waste management stocks, continued to perform well in the face of economic uncertainty and market volatility given the consistency of its business model.

RBC Bearings, Inc. (RBC)

RBC is a manufacturer and marketer of highly engineered precision bearings that are sold in the Industrial ( 70% of Revenue) and the Aerospace markets ( 30% of Revenue). RBC's quarterly results and guidance were better than expected with an impressive gross margin performance as the company continued to achieve synergies from its Dodge acquisition. Visibility is improving for RBC due to the aerospace cycle and improvement in the industrial market (infrastructure bill).

Small Cap Composite - 1Q25 Bottom 5 Laggards

AAON, Inc. (AAON)

AAON's 4Q24 results and 2025 financial guidance was significantly below the market's expectations. The company's results were adversely impacted by the transition in the HVAC industry to a new refrigerant that requires a new building code. This transition caused a pause in orders during the 4Q as customers worked through old inventory. The company also had manufacturing inefficiencies in its Washington BASX facility and significant expansion expenses for its liquid cooling facility in Memphis, TN. We believe these issues are temporary in nature and while there may be a quarter or two of choppiness, AAON's medium and long-term prospects remain solid.

SPS Commerce, Inc. (SPSC)

Shares declined by 14% on the date SPSC announced fourth quarter earnings. New CEO Chad Collins has been active in acquiring technologies to help SPSC broadens its set of offerings. This coincides with a moderate deceleration in organic growth that has investors questioning management's revenue growth target and, more specifically, how much will be organic. SPSC has historically grown through a combination of wallet share expansion and new customer additions. While typically balanced, this has shifted to mostly wallet share growth, raising another question with investors around new logo acquisition.

Construction Partners, Inc. (ROAD)

After being one of the portfolio's leading contributor in six of the past seven quarters, shares in ROAD pulled back during the first quarter of 2025. This was likely due to some profittaking and general weakness in infrastructure stocks after strong gains in 2024. ROAD was also the subject of a short report in late January, which we found to be lacking in terms of new information. ROAD reported strong fiscal first quarter results, with the quarter well ahead of estimates and backlog again hitting a record. Management raised guidance for the year, a positive sign that the strong demand it is witnessing should continue.

Vertex, Inc. (VERX)

VERX is a market leader in providing tax compliance software that helps corporations determine the correct amount of indirect taxes such as sales taxes and value added taxes. The stock corrected after they announced some targeted investments in new products that could further accelerate growth going forward. After recently attending VERX's first ever analyst day, we are confident in the company's outlook.

Descartes Systems Group, Inc. (DSGX)

DSGX is a leading provider of cloud-based logistics and supply chain solutions with over 26,000 customers worldwide. After being a leading contributor to portfolio return last quarter, DSGX traded lower on in-line quarterly results that revealed a deceleration in organic revenue growth. In addition, guidance was more conservative due to the number of crosscurrents in the supply chain and logistics market. DSGX should be well positioned as tariffs and increasing global trading complexity necessitates the need for the technology solutions DSGX offers.

Small Cap Composite - 1Q25 Buys*

None.

Small Cap Composite - 1Q25 Sells*

Altair Engineering, Inc. (ALTR)

ALTR, a leader in design and simulation software, announced on October 31, 2024, that it was being acquired by Siemens for $113 per share, representing an equity value of $10.6 billion. The price represented a 19% premium to the stock's unaffected market price on October 21st and a 13% premium to the stock's all-time high closing price. The deal values ALTR at approximately 14x 2025 revenue and 63x EBITDA. The deal closed March 26, 2025.

Paycor HCM, Inc. (PYCR)

PYCR is a leading provider of payroll and HCM software focused on middle-market customers. In early January 2025, the stock rallied significantly after Bloomberg reported that Paychex (PAYX) was in advanced negotiations to acquire PYCR in an all-cash transaction for $4.1 B. This equates to $22.50 per share, a 22% premium vs its prior close. With Apax Partners' controlling >50% stake in PYCR, we elected to realize gains and reinvest into higher conviction holdings rather than wait for the deal to close.

Conestoga added positions on eight occasions and trimmed stocks on three occasions during the first quarter.

data.title: Meituan: Capturing Brazil's Food Delivery Goldmine With Keeta
data.description:
At the recent “China-Brazil Business Seminar”, Meituan (OTCPK:MPNGF) announced that it plans to launch its overseas delivery platform, Keeta, in Brazil and pledges $1bn in investment in the next five years. This will be Keeta’s third ex-China destination following success in Saudi Arabia and Hong Kong.

Although we suspected that Keeta would pick an international destination that is closer to China given MT’s brand equity in the region and amongst Chinese users who have migrated to developed region such as Singapore, MT’s decision to expand into Brazil underscores its ability to execute on non-Asia regions on the back of its success in the GCC.

We reiterate our bullish view on Meituan. Our thesis on MT is that the company could be one of the few Chinese internet companies that has the potential to expand its footprint beyond China to offset China’s macro deceleration and market maturity by replicating its competitive moat in other emerging markets. Meituan has proven its overseas execution in the GCC, particularly in Saudi Arabia, where it is expanding beyond food delivery (see: Meituan: Riding On Keeta To Drive Overseas Growth) and in Hong Kong where it has successfully taken share from global peers (see: Meituan: On-Demand Momentum, Eyeing Lucrative Overseas Growth And Navigating AI Investments).

Although there were some concerns of rising competition in China on the back of JD’s entry into food delivery, we believe this competitive dynamic is manageable and that MT is likely to retain its leadership in food delivery, while JD could gain incremental share from a subscale player such as Alibaba’s Eleme.

We believe that MT can continue to replicate its success in Brazil, similar to how it captured a significant market share in the GCC and Hong Kong. On the back of an easing in the Sino-US tension, we believe that the stock is attractively priced at 12.8x 2026E earnings and should remain a long-term holding for any investor looking to gain a “China+” (China plus global growth) exposure.

A large and under-tapped market

The food delivery market in Brazil is projected to grow at a 15% CAGR in the next 8 years to reach $4.5bn by 2033, one of the fastest-growing markets in the region due to low food delivery penetration and rising merchant and consumer digitization.

The market is highly concentrated, with iFood dominating with a 70%-80% market share after Uber folded the market in 2022. Another major player is Rappi, which holds around 7% of the market share.

The heavy concentration by one player indicates potential for disruption, in our view. If we were to look at China, MT’s home market, JD’s recent entry into food delivery illustrates the arbitrage opportunity that a new entrant could capitalize on. Notably, gig workers in China are uninsured and lack benefits. JD’s proposition of full-time employee benefits for full-time riders, low merchant commissions, and the focus on licensed merchants allowed JD to capture some market share in the beginning, as it was widely viewed as socially responsible for both employees and consumers.

MT could leverage a similar tactic to be competitive in Brazil. We note that a typical commission rate for restaurants charged by iFoods is around 25%, with an average food delivery time of around 30-40 minutes. MT could offer lower commission to merchants to drive supply-side economics while leveraging its big data analytics to lower delivery time and offer a superior user experience to drive the demand-side economics.

Keeta’s differentiation

MT has announced that it is committing $1bn in the next five years to expand in Brazil. Much of the investment will be devoted to subsidies for both merchants and consumers, tech integration, and labor expansion, as MT has planned to hire 50k riders in the next 12 months.

Looking at the case studies from the GCC and HK, MT’s ability to expand market share in a short period of time was largely due to aggressive subsidies, localization of payment options and ensuring a consistent experience.

Similar tactics could be deployed in several areas of Brazil. On the merchant side, lower commission as we have mentioned, could drive supply-side scale. Assuming MT lowers merchant commission to 10-20% compared with iFood’s 25%, a typical merchant could save around $500/month which could be significant for smaller-scale merchants.

On the consumer side, MT has offered aggressive coupons and promotions to both GCC and HK-based consumers and the promotions for the Brazilian consumer should not be any less attractive, in our view.

Finally, rider bonus on timely delivery and delivery volume per day could drive some rider churn from iFoods to join MT as gig workers look to maximize earnings.

When we look at the geographic priority of focusing on Sao Paulo’s Villa Olimpia tech hub, this is very similar to that of rollout in Hong Kong where MT initially focused on a controlled area in Mongkok and Tai Kok Tsui to ensure execution consistency and testing before nationwide rollout.

Incremental revenue impact

We are bullish on MT’s entry into Brazil and believe that MT could take a meaningful share, translating to long-term upside surprises to the current consensus.

As we noted above, Brazil’s food delivery market is estimated to be $1.29bn with an expected 15% CAGR. Assuming Meituan captures 30% of the market, this would translate to $512mn in GTV share.

Assume that MT charges a blended 15% commission on food delivery and marketing services; this would translate to $77mn in revenue. Converting to Hong Kong dollars, we estimate that Keeta Brazil alone could have a 30bps revenue impact on MT.

Keep in mind that Brazil, along with GCC and Hong Kong are just one of many ex-China locations that MT can expand on and we believe that overtime ex-China revenue contribution could reach 10% of MT’s total shares in the next five-10 years, which is why we remain bullish on MT and continue to be positive on its ex-China execution as it looks to grow beyond China.

data.title: VIXY: The Fear Has Subsided (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

This year has been marked by a massive spike in volatility, move which tanked equities but also produced a rise in the price of long volatility products. We last covered one of these instruments in the beginning of April 2025, when we rated the ProShares VIX Short-Term Futures ETF (BATS:VIXY) a 'Strong Sell'. We usually do not attach 'Strong Buy' or 'Strong Sell' ratings unless it is a very high conviction trade. And selling VIXY was one:

The name is down -32% since our rating, retracing most of its move up. We find this instrument to be a very interesting one, falling in the short trades category, rather than a buy and hold. Given the move lower in volatility we believe the above trade is done, with investors being well-served in covering any shorts here and moving to 'Hold' (i.e. neutral). We are going to highlight in this article the mechanics of the risk factors involved, and why we believe the move lower in VIXY is now exhausted.

The VIX is back under 20

The 'fear gauge' or VIX, is now back under 20 after spiking as high as 50:

Please note how 'clean' the above chart is. The VIX is usually hugging the 15 handle in a normalized environment, with all the spikes higher normalizing over time. This makes sense from a financial structuring standpoint because VIX simply represents the implied volatility of options out to 30-days, thus it needs to be constantly bid to stay high. If investors suddenly are not fearful anymore, VIX naturally comes down as puts are not bought anymore.

With VIX now at 18, things are back to a relative 'normal', thus the next 'surprise' move in VIX is actually for the upside. If things stay as they are the volatility index will stay range bound between 15 and 20, thus not offering much upside in being short VIXY.

The futures curve is back in contango

Kindly keep in mind though that VIXY is a play on VIX futures, rather than the index itself:

The S&P 500® VIX Short-Term Futures Index utilizes prices of the next two near-term VIX® futures contracts to replicate a position that rolls the nearest month VIX futures to the next month on a daily basis in equal fractional amounts. This results in a constant one-month rolling long position in first and second month VIX futures contracts.

If we take a look at the current fund composition on its website, we get the following:

Most of the name is now invested in June 2025 futures, while the rest is in the May contracts.

It is important to remember that the fund invests in futures because the shape of the futures curve matters as well. Currently we are back in contango:

Contango is when the longer dated VIX futures have a higher value than the front month one. You can notice the upward sloping blue curve (which is the VIX futures curve). In a normalized environment the VIX futures curve is in contango, hence less of an incentive to short VIXY.

When a significant risk-off event occurs, the curve shifts to backwardation because everybody rushes to buy short dated puts for portfolio protection. Steep backwardation is a green flag to short VIXY. Not anymore.

While the market still has a small level of fear (the blue curve is above spot VIX), the mechanics now are tilted to another bout of risk-off rather than further tightening. Please keep in mind that risk-off equates VIXY higher, thus you would want to be long.

Short-term trading instrument

We cannot say this enough, but VIXY is a short term trading instrument. This is not a buy and hold, and positioning has to have clear entry and exit points for trades in VIXY to work. You want to short VIXY when fear prevails and the VIX spikes, and you want to move to neutral when the market normalizes. Occasionally, if one believes there is too much complacency you can also go long, but we do not like that trade very much because of the long-term roll effect and the fact that the fund tends to lose significant value over long periods of time:

The name is down -86% in the past three years, but has massive swings in between. This is not a buy and hold instrument, or a sell and hold instrument for that matter. VIXY is an ETF to use for short-term trading patterns only (in our view).

With our trade thesis now materialized (we were pleading for a normalization in our prior article) we are upgrading the rating here to Hold (i.e. neutral).

Conclusion

VIXY is an exchange traded fund. The name represents a long position in VIX futures, and spikes in price when the market experiences risk-off events. We assigned the fund a 'Strong Sell' rating as fear peaked in April, and the name is down -32% since. With the VIX index now under 20 and the futures curve in contango, we are upgrading the fund to 'Hold' given the lack of further upside in our original trade idea.

data.title: Grab: Two Major Catalysts Ahead
data.description:
Introduction

Grab (NASDAQ:GRAB) stock has had a volatile couple of months. Shares fell 40% from their February highs due to mixed Q4 earnings and Trump's Liberation Day. However, as weeks go by and as trade deals begin to form, Grab (along with the broader markets) recouped all its initial losses, rallying by about 50% from its April lows.

While tariff uncertainties may keep the stock zigzagging for the next few months, I believe Grab is more recession-resistant and tariff-resistant than many people think, and thus, the company should maintain strong fundamentals in the face of an economic storm — management confirmed this during their recent Q1 earnings update.

As you know, the leading superapp in Southeast Asia has gained a lot of buzz lately, especially among the retail investing community. In addition, a barrage of speculative news had investors on the edge of their seats.

Having said that, I believe Grab stock is still undervalued at $5 a share. While I believe Grab stock is well on its way to new 52-week highs, there are two major catalysts that could shorten this path.

Growth: Watch Out For Lending Acceleration

Looking at its Q1 results, Grab generated a record Revenue of $773M, up 18% YoY, with growth accelerating 1pp QoQ. This was driven by growth across all segments. This also beat analyst estimates by $10M.

Record Revenue was also driven by record Monthly Transacting Users (MTUs). In Q1, MTUs were 44.5M, up 16% YoY. Despite the seasonal impacts from the Lunar New Year and Ramadan fasting period, Grab added 0.6M New MTUs QoQ. This speaks volumes about Grab's incredible value proposition as the number one superapp in all of Southeast Asia.

Importantly, despite being the leading superapp in the region, Grab's user penetration is still low, with MTUs representing only 6% of the Southeast Asian population. Additionally, Daily Transacting Users only make up 16% of its MTU base, pointing to opportunities to improve engagement and monetization rates. Furthermore, Grab estimated that there are 119M Annual Transacting Users as of Q1, highlighting the company's potential to grow its MTU base multiple times from here, just by reengaging with "old" customers.

On-Demand MTUs grew 18% YoY, with On-Demand transactions up 21% YoY. These drove an 18% YoY growth in On-Demand GMV to $4.9B.

  • Deliveries GMV was $3.1B, up 16% YoY, driven by higher Deliveries transactions and Deliveries MTUs, which grew 17% YoY. Notably, Saver Deliveries now accounts for 35% of Deliveries transactions, up 9pp YoY, and Group Orders GMV grew over 300% YoY.
  • Mobility GMV was $1.8B, up 17% YoY, driven by higher Mobility transactions and Mobility MTUs, which grew 25% YoY and 20% YoY, respectively. A key highlight from the segment was Advance Booking GMV growing by over 200% YoY.

Looking at the table above, notice the slowdown in growth for both segments. This was due to "the Ramadan fasting period this year fully occurring within the first quarter, compared to 2024, where Ramadan occurred over both the first and second quarters". As such, we can expect a nice acceleration in On-Demand GMV growth in Q2 as YoY comps will be easier in Q2 — management confirmed this in their earnings update, citing a "strong demand recovery in April".

As a result of robust On-Demand GMV growth, Deliveries Revenue grew 19% YoY to $415M while Mobility Revenue grew 14% YoY to $282M.

Also worth mentioning is that Deliveries Revenue growth accelerated 6pp QoQ, primarily due to lower Deliveries Incentives of $350M, as compared to $363M in Q4, as well as higher Advertising Revenue.

Grab's Advertising business continues its upward trajectory, with Monthly Active Advertisers up 49% YoY and average spend from these advertisers up 30% YoY. While Advertising Revenue as a % of Deliveries GMV was flat QoQ (likely due to a seasonally softer advertising demand in Q1), the metric improved 40bps YoY to 1.7% in Q1, signifying improved ad take rates.

I can attest to this as I have noticed plenty of advertising activity, from the app's home page to car wraps and even the app's delivery tracking/navigation system (GrabMaps). Excitingly, Grab's advertising business is still lowly penetrated, indicating a long growth runway ahead. For instance, self-serve Monthly Active Advertisers were 191K as of Q1, which is only a 3% penetration rate against its merchant/partner ecosystem of about 6M.

As the advertising business continues to grow, it should contribute to high-margin Revenue growth, boosting the company's overall profits in the process.

Financial Services Revenue was $75M, up 36% YoY, driven by the lending and insurance units. Interestingly, Financial Services Revenue was up only $1M QoQ — this also happened in the same quarter last year, so I reckon Q1 to be a seasonally slow quarter for the fintech business. As such, we can expect an acceleration in Financial Services Revenue throughout the remainder of the year.

Nevertheless, the fintech business continues to display strong growth in Q1:

  • Loan Portfolio was $566M, up 56% YoY.
  • Loans Disbursed were $630M, up 30% YoY.
  • Digibank Deposits were $1.4B, up 199% YoY.

All things considered, management reiterated their full-year Revenue guidance of $3.33B to $3.40B, implying a 19% to 22% YoY growth. This also means that we can expect growth rates to accelerate over Q1's Revenue growth of 18%. Here are some of the initiatives that should drive growth in the short-to-medium term:

  • New products launched during the GrabX product event, including GrabFood For One (food delivery for solo diners), Shared Saver (group order with people nearby), and Dine Out Discovery (enhanced restaurant discovery and booking feature).
  • The launch of GrabCab in Singapore, a new street-hailing service fully operated by Grab.
  • The rollout of autonomous vehicles in Southeast Asia, following its new partnerships with four autonomous technology companies: Autonomous A2Z, Motional, WeRide, and Zelos.

While these are exciting projects for Grab, I think there's one area of Grab's business that could potentially take off: the fintech lending business.

It's important to note that the lending business is currently concentrated on Grab's driver-partners. However, this part of the business seems to be plateauing.

To fertilize its next stage of growth, management intends to ramp up consumer lending through its Digibanks (GXS Bank, GX Bank, and Superbank). By the same token, management plans to extend the lending business to more merchants in the future.

So in 2025, an increasing proportion of the lending will be to consumers, but we'll continue to deepen and enhance the relationship we have with lending to partners. I would say our penetration of drivers is the highest. We're still improving the models there and working out ways to facilitate smaller quanta and different tenures to more drivers. But I think that is starting to plateau. And therefore, that will grow only at the rate at which the number of drivers and the GMV they support comes on to the marketplace.

In terms of merchants, we are much less penetrated. And as we develop more and more capabilities for merchants like enhancing our payments enhancing our dine discovery which is the second part of your question that allows us to see more of their GMV and allows us therefore to extend our credit models and extend more lending to them.

(COO Alex Hungate — Grab FY2025 Q1 Earnings Call, emphasis added)

In other words, Grab's lending exposure to consumers and merchants is still very low, and now that all three Digibanks have loan products in the marketplace, Grab is ready to expand its consumer and merchant lending businesses, which, I believe, should generate extreme Revenue growth for the segment since consumers and merchants are much larger markets — demographically and financially — than drivers.

The growth of the lending business should be supercharged by higher cross-sell rates across the Grab ecosystem. For one, over 60% of its user base are now using two or more Grab offerings. Additionally, the majority of new Financial Services users are existing users of Grab's On-Demand services.

In the future, management also plans to expand GrabUnlimited — a premium subscription service that offers users additional Deliveries and Mobility benefits — to Financial Services.

As Grab continues to drive cross-sales across its ecosystem and as more users adopt GrabUnlimited, the Grab ecosystem becomes even more sticky, with each user generating even higher sales for the company.

For context, Grab's Revenue Per MTU has been hovering around $17 over the last two years, implying a low monetization rate. That said, through the combination of new product launches, higher cross-selling rates, and the expansion of the lending business, I expect this metric to explode in the coming years, delivering solid Revenue growth in the process.

Profitability: Raised Guidance Signifies Strong Operating Leverage

Jumping to the bottom of the income statement, Q1 Net Income was $10M, improving from $(115)M a year ago. Net Margin as a % of Revenue improved 19pp YoY, from (18)% to 1%. Higher Revenue, higher Net Finance Income, and lower Share-based Compensation drove this improvement.

EPS was $0.01, inline with estimates.

Looking at the profitability of each segment, the On-Demand segments saw sequential improvements while the Financial Services segment continued to burn cash as it is still in the early phases of growth:

  • Deliveries Adjusted EBITDA was $63M at a 2.0% Margin as a % of GMV, up 40bps YoY and 20bps QoQ. This was driven by increased contributions from Advertising and continued operating leverage in the segment. Though management is confident that they'll achieve their long-term Deliveries Margin of 4%+, they are currently prioritizing "absolute dollar expansion, EBITDA expansion versus a certain margin percentage", so expect margins to continue to fluctuate in the near term. The good news is that management is already seeing 4%+ Margins in certain countries, confirming the segment's potential to expand Margins to that 4% level.
  • Mobility Adjusted EBITDA was $159M at an 8.8% Margin as a % of GMV. While Margins dropped 10bps YoY, it improved 40bps QoQ. The YoY decline was driven by higher incentives to grow its driver supply base. On the other side, operating leverage within the segment led to a nice QoQ improvement in Margins.
  • Financial Services Adjusted EBITDA was $(30)M at a (41)% Margin as a % of Revenue. This was mainly due to "an increase in expected credit loss provisions as we continued to increase our loan disbursals". However, as the lending business explodes in the coming quarters, I expect this segment to turn a profit as it delivers serious efficiency gains from scaling.

In aggregate, Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA was $192M at a 3.8% Margin as a % of GMV, which improved 20bps YoY and 10bps QoQ.

Subtracting Regional Operating Costs of $86M, which improved from $91M last year, Grab produced a record Adjusted EBITDA of $106M in Q1, with Margins, both as a % of GMV and Revenue, improving significantly on a YoY basis.

As a result of strong growth and profitability performance in Q1, management is raising their profit guidance for the year, now expecting 2025 Adjusted EBITDA of $460M to $480M, up from $440M to $470M previously. In addition, management reiterated Financial Services Adjusted EBITDA breakeven by the second half of 2026 — I think they'll achieve this in the first half.

All things considered, Grab is displaying strong operating leverage, with the raised guidance as a vote of confidence. I expect this to continue in the ensuing years as Grab scales further.

Health: $5.9B of Net Cash But No Buybacks...

Q1 Adjusted Free Cash Flow was $(101)M, down from $(94)M last year and $69M from the previous quarter. This was due to higher working capital outflows typical of the first quarter, as well as higher capital expenditures.

On a TTM basis, however, Adjusted FCF is still positive, at $157M. This represents a TTM Adjusted FCF Margin of 5%, which improved 10pp YoY.

That said, one of the most interesting parts of the earnings report is that management did not repurchase any shares in Q1.

One potential reason for its zero buyback activity in Q1 is that Grab is gearing up for a major acquisition. The company has recently acquired Chope (a restaurant booking platform), Everise (a premium supermarket chain), and most recently, Validus (an all-in-one SME financing platform), so Grab is no stranger to the M&A world.

As you may or may not know, rumors are circulating that Grab is pursuing an acquisition of its biggest competitor in Indonesia, GoTo, the company behind GoJek and Tokopedia.

While this speculation has been ongoing over the last few years, let me tell you why I think this time is different:

  • This is the first quarter since the company authorized its $500M buyback program in Q4 of 2023, during which Grab did not buy back any shares. This is despite having $5.9B of Net Cash Liquidity, $274M of buyback capacity left, and the stock still down 70% from its highs.
  • In the past, GoTo has always denied merger talks with Grab. This time round, GoTo did not confirm or deny reports of a potential merger with Grab, only stating that "no agreement is in place between the Company and any party to enter into a transaction of the type that has been speculated on in the mass media".
  • Per Reuters, Grab has held discussions with several banks to act as funding partners. The acquisition value is rumored to be about $7B, and since Grab has only $5.9B of Net Cash, Grab would likely need to take out a loan to finance the acquisition. There were two separate sources with knowledge of this.

  • Grab has recently added Steven Tishman to the Board of Directors. Mr. Tishman has over 40 years of experience in investment banking and capital markets, specializing in... M&A deals. Coincidence? I don't think so.

Given these four developments over the past few months, I'd give it a high probability that a Grab-GoTo merger happens this year. From a fundamental standpoint, this deal would be highly synergistic, at least as it relates to the On-Demand segment (Gojek). This consolidation will ultimately cement Grab's leadership position in one of the most important markets in Southeast Asia: Indonesia.

But regardless of whether the deal goes through or not, I'm confident that Grab will still be a dominant force in the region for decades to come.

Valuation: Still Cheap

Quickly looking at the company's valuation, Grab is trading at an EV to Revenue of 5.3x, which is still attractive given the company's improving fundamentals, massive growth runway in Southeast Asia, and 30% Net Cash position.

As for me, I have a 12-month base-case price target of $7.15, which is relatively unchanged from my previous article. This represents an upside potential of about 41% from its current price of $5.06.

This assumes the following:

  • 2034 Deliveries GMV of $33.3B (11% CAGR) at a 4.5% Margin, inline with management's long-term margin guidance.
  • 2034 Mobility GMV of $24.1B (14% CAGR) at a 9.5% Margin, inline with management's long-term margin guidance.
  • 2034 Financial Services Revenue of $2.4B (25% CAGR) at a 35% Margin.
  • Altogether, this should produce Cash Flows (Adjusted EBITDA - CapEx) of $2.9B by 2034.
  • Perpetual Growth Rate of 2.5%.
  • Discount Rate of 10%.

I have also provided my bear and bull cases below.

As I've highlighted throughout this article, two major catalysts to watch out for are: 1) the acceleration of the Financial Services segment, and 2) the official news of a Grab-GoTo merger.

Risks

Recession

As you know, tariff talks are causing a lot of uncertainty across global markets. Unfavorable developments in trade deals may cause an economic slowdown, which may pressure Grab's growth as consumers pare back spending. However, I would argue that Grab is more recession-resistant than many would've thought, especially considering all the new, affordable services that just went live on the platform.

We are confident that the strong execution of our affordable and viral product- and tech-led strategies will increasingly position us as a counter-cyclical company, enabling us to remain resilient in an uncertain macroeconomic environment as we drive new user growth, improvements to usage frequency and user retention.

(CEO Anthony Tan — FY2025 Q1 Grab Earnings Call, emphasis added)

Growth Slowdown

While I believe Grab has a long growth runway ahead, one of my big concerns is that Grab's growth may slow down meaningfully if the company reduces incentives paid out to its consumers and its partners. There is no clear sign that this is happening just yet, since the company is still maintaining high incentives to drive growth, but this is one thing to be wary of in the future.

Thesis

As seen by its strong Q1 results, Grab's fundamentals continue to improve, with the company growing MTUs, Revenue, and Adjusted EBITDA to record highs.

Excitingly, Grab still has so much room for growth as it has a low penetration rate of just 6% and a low monetization rate of just $17 per MTU. This is why management is still investing heavily in growth, pumping high incentives and launching new products to maximize product adoption and user growth.

Meanwhile, at just $5 a share, the stock is still trading below intrinsic value, offering decent upside potential for long-term investors.

Moreover, investors can look forward to the eventual explosion of the lending business and the potential Grab-GoTo merger, both of which could serve as a major catalyst that'll launch the stock to new highs.
